script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. Wednesday — here is your QQQM recap. The portfolio closed one point eight one percent up on the session. Broad strength in mega-cap technology carried the day, though a sharp divergence inside the semiconductor space kept things complicated for holders.

Micron Technology dropped more than eight percent in the afternoon session. The catalyst: intensifying competition from a major Chinese rival and rising expectations of weaker memory chip pricing. That is a position representing nearly six percent of this portfolio, and it moved hard against the tape.

The Nasdaq-100 led the major indices Wednesday, driven by outsized gains in large-cap technology and communication services names. Alphabet and Nvidia both posted moves well above four percent, providing meaningful upside ballast to offset losses in the memory space. No Federal Reserve speakers or major economic prints crossed the wire during the session, leaving equities to trade primarily on earnings positioning and company-specific news flow.

Micron Technology is the story of Wednesday's session, and it demands full context. Shares closed at nine hundred four dollars and twenty-eight cents, down three dollars and forty-nine percent on the session, but the intraday low reflected a loss closer to eight percent before a partial recovery into the close. The pressure came from two directions simultaneously. First, a report out of the memory supply chain indicated that ASML's highest-end lithography equipment is enabling manufacturers to produce memory chips faster than previously expected — a supply-side development that puts downward pressure on pricing. Second, and more significantly, competitive concern around a Chinese rival intensified, raising questions about long-term market share in commodity memory segments. Analysts covering the space noted that SK Hynix also surrendered gains Wednesday after a twenty-seven percent surge, suggesting the pressure is sector-wide rather than company-specific. For holders, Micron is scheduled to participate in the KeyBanc Capital Markets Technology Leadership Forum on August tenth, which may serve as the next meaningful opportunity for management to address the pricing and competition narrative directly.

Turning to Nvidia, which closed at two hundred twelve dollars and fifty cents, up four point four one percent on the session. The standout development for Nvidia on Wednesday is the China data center story. According to reporting from Yahoo Finance, Nvidia's first H-two hundred shipments into China are unlikely to move the needle on quarterly results in isolation — volumes are simply too low for that. What matters is the baseline: Nvidia's current guidance for the first quarter of fiscal twenty-twenty-seven assumes zero data center compute revenue from China. Any actual shipments represent optionality that is not priced into guidance, which gives analysts reason to see upside to estimates. Separately, a piece on Seeking Alpha flagged Nvidia's proprietary approach to the tokens-per-watt efficiency challenge, arguing the company's CMX offload architecture addresses idle accelerator cycles in a way competitors have not yet matched. The position is the largest in this portfolio at seven point six percent, and Wednesday's move added meaningfully.

Over at Apple, the company closed at three hundred twenty-seven dollars and fifty cents, up three point two one percent on the session. The primary news touching Apple Wednesday was indirect — Broadcom's relationship with Apple on custom silicon was highlighted in coverage examining Broadcom's valuation after an approximately eight-times five-year run. Taiwan Semiconductor is expected to report record second-quarter earnings Thursday, with analysts projecting a fifty-nine percent surge in net profit driven by three-nanometer and two-nanometer demand. Apple, as one of TSMC's largest customers, is directly tied to that supply chain. Watch the TSMC print Thursday morning.

Alphabet closed at three hundred seventy dollars and ninety-two cents, up five point two two percent on the session — one of the strongest moves in the portfolio Wednesday. The headline driver: Warren Buffett was quoted saying Alphabet will outperform ninety-five percent of Wall Street stock picks, with Berkshire Hathaway's stake now reported above thirty-one billion dollars. Institutional endorsement at that scale carries weight in the market. Alphabet also appeared in coverage examining Amazon's relative valuation versus hyperscaler peers, with Jefferies naming Amazon a top pick while noting Alphabet's positioning in AI infrastructure.

Microsoft closed at three hundred ninety-five dollars and sixty-three cents, up one point one nine percent on the session. The move was modest relative to peers but directionally consistent with the broader technology tape. No company-specific catalysts crossed Wednesday. The position at four point five two percent of the portfolio contributed positively to the session.

Amazon closed at two hundred fifty-four dollars and ninety-six cents, up three point zero nine percent on the session. Jefferies coverage published Wednesday explicitly called Amazon a top pick among hyperscaler names, citing a more attractive entry point relative to Walmart and Alphabet for gaining exposure to AI infrastructure and retail simultaneously. That analyst framing is worth monitoring as earnings season approaches.

Advanced Micro Devices closed at five hundred twenty-nine dollars and fourteen cents, down zero point nine eight percent on the session. The loss was relatively contained given the pressure across parts of the semiconductor space. No company-specific news drove the move. The position tracks the broader AI chip narrative closely, and the divergence from Nvidia on Wednesday is a data point worth watching as the two continue to compete in data center GPU deployments.

Tesla closed at three hundred ninety-four dollars and forty-six cents, down zero point zero eight percent on the session — essentially flat. The move was negligible, and no material company-specific news crossed Wednesday. The position at three point zero nine percent of the portfolio was a non-factor in either direction.

Intel closed at one hundred two dollars and ninety-nine cents, down zero point one three percent on the session. Like Tesla, the move was minimal. Intel continues to trade in a different context than its semiconductor peers — more turnaround story than AI infrastructure play — and Wednesday's quiet session reflects that separation from the broader tape.

Three data points to watch in the next forty-eight hours. First, Taiwan Semiconductor reports second-quarter earnings Thursday morning. With analysts projecting a fifty-nine percent surge in net profit, the result and management commentary on advanced node demand will carry direct implications for Nvidia, Apple, and AMD, all of which depend on TSMC manufacturing capacity. Second, GE Aerospace also reports Thursday, flagged in Wednesday's coverage alongside Taiwan Semi — less directly relevant to this portfolio, but a read on industrial and infrastructure capital spending. Third, Micron's participation in the KeyBanc Technology Leadership Forum on August tenth is now on the calendar, but watch for any pre-event management commentary or analyst preview notes that begin to surface in the days ahead given Wednesday's sharp pricing and competition headlines.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. The Taiwan Semiconductor earnings print Thursday morning is the single most consequential near-term event for this portfolio — the supply chain read-through touches nearly every major position across the book.
